flashcards.back: The field of computer science dealing with creating, manipulating, and representing visual content using computers. It involves hardware and software techniques to generate images from data.

flashcards.front: What is CAD in Computer Graphics?
flashcards.back: Computer-Aided Design uses graphics software to create, modify, and optimize designs in engineering, architecture, and product design. It provides precision and simulation capabilities.
Image representation using a grid of pixels, where each pixel contains color information. Examples include BMP, JPEG, PNG. Resolution determines quality.
Image representation using mathematical equations to define lines, curves, and shapes. Scalable without quality loss. Examples include SVG, AI, EPS files.
Computer-Generated Imagery (CGI) creates still or animated visual content using computer graphics. Used extensively in films for special effects and virtual environments.
A dedicated memory area in graphics hardware that stores pixel data to be displayed on screen. Controls refresh rate and color depth of display.
Technique to smooth jagged edges in digital images by blending pixel colors at boundaries. Reduces stair-stepping effect in rendered graphics.
A depth-sorting algorithm in 3D graphics that determines visible surfaces by storing depth values. Prevents hidden surface problems in rendering.
Rendering technique that simulates light rays traveling through a scene. Creates photorealistic images by tracing paths of light from camera.
3D rendering method where surfaces are represented as polygons (usually triangles). Fast and efficient for real-time applications like games.
Applying 2D images (textures) onto 3D surfaces to add detail and realism without increasing polygon count. Uses UV coordinates for mapping.

flashcards.front: What is a Graphical User Interface (GUI)?
flashcards.back: Interface using visual elements like windows, icons, menus for user interaction. Replaced text-based interfaces making computers accessible to non-technical users.
Immersive technology using computer graphics to create simulated 3D environments. Users interact naturally with virtual worlds through specialized devices.
Using computer graphics to represent scientific data visually. Helps researchers understand complex data through charts, graphs, and 3D visualizations.
Sequence of steps to convert 3D scene descriptions into 2D images. Includes vertex processing, rasterization, fragment processing, and frame buffer operations.
